strategy_logic.core_hypothesis: Three common factors (market excess return, SMB, HML) explain most of the cross-sectional variation in stock and bond returns, challenging the single-factor CAPM
strategy_logic.market_logic: Stocks with similar factor loadings (size and value characteristics) exhibit systematic comovement. Small-cap stocks and high book-to-market (value) stocks command risk premiums that are not captured by market beta alone
strategy_logic.alpha_source: Abnormal returns (alphas) capture residual performance not explained by factor exposures. After accounting for market, size, and value factors, portfolio alphas approach zero, suggesting factors explain most return variation
strategy_logic.applicable_conditions: Works best in markets with well-developed stock exchanges (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ). Factor premiums (SMB, HML) showed historical significance from 1963-1991 but may weaken post-publication due to increased arbitrage activity

data_requirements.data_source: CRSP (stock returns, market cap), Compustat (book value, B/M calculation), FRED/Federal Reserve (bond yields, risk-free rate)

operation_steps.signal_generation: Sort stocks by market cap (median split) and B/M ratio (30th/70th percentiles) to create 6 portfolios: S/L, S/M, S/H, B/L, B/M, B/H. Factor returns: SMB = (S/L+S/M+S/H)/3 - (B/L+B/M+B/H)/3; HML = (S/H+B/H)/2 - (S/L+B/L)/2
operation_steps.position_sizing: Equal-weighted or value-weighted within factor portfolios. Long portfolios with desired factor exposure (e.g., small-cap value), short opposite portfolios (e.g., large-cap growth)
operation_steps.rebalance_frequency: Annual rebalancing recommended based on June-end size and B/M rankings (standard academic convention), though quarterly or semi-annual also acceptable
operation_steps.stop_loss: Not explicitly specified in paper; academic implementation typically does not include stop-loss rules
operation_steps.transaction_cost: Paper assumes realistic trading costs; factor portfolios with moderate turnover (annual rebalancing) maintain profitability after transaction costs

model_framework.framework: Fama-French Three-Factor Model: R_i,t - Rf,t = α_i + β_i(Rm,t - Rf,t) + s_i SMB_t + h_i HML_t + ε_i,t
model_framework.validation: Cross-sectional regressions, time-series regression of portfolio returns on factors, R² comparison between CAPM and three-factor models

strengths: Explains 90%+ of diversified portfolio return variation
Statistically significant factor premiums (MKT: 4.8%, SMB: 3.0%, HML: 5.0% annualized)
Applicable to both stocks and bonds
weaknesses: Factor decay post-publication as arbitrage activity increased
Data mining concerns (out-of-sample performance may be weaker)
Economic mechanism (risk vs. mispricing) not fully resolved
Factor premiums can reverse during crises (growth outperforms, large caps outperform)
Hundreds of proposed factors create 'factor zoo' problem
improvement_directions: Five-factor model (add RMW profitability, CMA investment factors)
Machine learning approaches for factor discovery
Conditional factor models accounting for regime changes

datasets.name: CRSP-Compustat Merged Database (NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ stocks)
datasets.source: Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP), Standard & Poor's Compustat
datasets.time_range: July 1963 - December 1991 (sample period in original paper)
datasets.processing: Stocks sorted by market cap (June-end) and B/M (fiscal year-end with lag). Bond portfolios formed by maturity and credit quality. Monthly portfolio returns computed from stock-level data

known_limitations: Factor premiums have weakened in recent decades
Survivorship bias concerns in historical data
Look-ahead bias if not properly accounting for data availability
assumption_risks: Factors represent true risk premia (not fully proven)
Stationarity of factor relationships over time
Factor loadings remain stable (may shift in different market regimes)
No transaction costs assumed in theoretical framework
implementation_gaps: Theory does not specify rebalancing frequency - must be chosen empirically
Factor construction details (sorting thresholds) may vary
Data vintage and accounting standards change over time
Tax implications not addressed in academic paper

references.original_paper: Fama, E.F. and French, K.R. (1993). Common risk factors in the returns on stocks and bonds. Journal of Financial Economics, 33(1), 3-56.
related_papers: Fama, E.F. and French, K.R. (2015). A five-factor asset pricing model. Journal of Financial Economics, 116(1), 1-22.
Hou, K., Xue, C., and Zhang, L. (2015). Digesting anomalies: An investment approach. Review of Financial Studies, 28(3), 650-705.
Carhart, M.M. (1997). On persistence in mutual fund performance. Journal of Finance, 52(1), 57-82.
Markowitz, H. (1952). Portfolio Selection. Journal of Finance, 7(1), 77-91.
code_repositories: Fama-French factors available for download in CSV format from above source

suggested_signal.reasoning: The Fama-French three-factor model is the foundational factor pricing model. Factor ranking (sorting stocks by size and value, then long-short portfolios) directly implements the paper's methodology. Historical evidence shows significant factor premiums (HML: 5.0% annualized, SMB: 3.0% annualized) with high statistical significance (t-stats > 2.8). While factor decay is a concern, the model remains one of the most robust and well-validated frameworks in empirical asset pricing.
